So you want to figure that out. You want to be selling, if you can, to people who are invested. And you are leveraging the way they are already, financially, or emotionally, or relationship by some means invested. You want to be selling to them. And you want to leverage the investment. See, most people, a big thing to remember is most people's psychology and most people's behavior is the exact opposite of how 5% and 1% behave in the same circumstances. So if you don't know how a crowd is going to behave, just think about how the most successful person in that crowd would behave. And that ain't it. So what do highly successful people do with just about everything? Swiff sword. Ain't working off with its head. Next. Not giving me what I want off with its head. Next. That's why they like direct response. Spend a dollar, didn't get $1.20. I'm not doing that anymore. How do most people behave? Real slow sword. Real slow. Slow motion. You go see a bad movie. It's two hours and 15 minutes long. And you know 10 minutes in, this thing sucks. I mean, it's bad. Right? So how do you behave? Do you leave? Many of you will. Now, you'll be less likely to leave if you have somebody with you than if you went by yourself in the afternoon. And if you went to dinner and went to the movie with another couple, you will be a lot less likely to leave. I went to a Notre Dame game. I always wanted to go to a Notre Dame game. So a friend of mine is a professor at Notre Dame. Herb True. So it takes me to a Notre Dame game. I mean, we got good seats. And it's like 40 below with 40 mile an hour winds. I mean, it is just miserable. People are literally getting coffee. The beer guy is selling coffee. He's going around with coffee. They are taking the lid off. I swear to God, and pouring it on their hands. Right? I mean, which the minute I saw it, it's smart. I mean, I am dying. But Herb got the tickets. I'm there with Herb. I went all the way to Notre Dame to go to this game. Mostly, I don't want to be rude to him. Right? Finally, halftime, which by the way, Notre Dame is killing whoever they were playing too. So it isn't even a game. I mean, finally at halftime, I just said, Herb, I can't take it anymore. We got to go back to your house. So what's the rest of this on TV? I mean, I'm a popsicle. Yeah? He says, thank God. Yeah? He says, I wanted to leave an hour ago, but you know, you came all this way. I know you want to go. I'm like, you know, let's get the hell out of here. So, so, what if it is not the worst movie ever made? It is one of the 10 worst movies ever made. It is called That Long Last Love, and it stars Burt Reynolds and Sybil Shepard, and it's a musical. Need you hear more. But this is at a time when, you know, Burt was a big deal, and if Burt Reynolds was in a movie, Burt Reynolds fans, we went and saw it, right? So Pete Lullo, Pete DePernar and I go to see That Long Last Love in the middle of the day by ourselves, right? But we've driven to the theater, and we got our popcorn, and we got, you know, our stuff. And on comes this movie, and I mean, it is shockingly horrible. It's like, un-king-believable, right? Un-believable. There's only about another 20 people there, and within a half an hour, it's just us. All of them have left. We keep looking at each other and saying, it's got to get better. Optimistic bias, right? Because it's Burt Reynolds, and we like Burt. We say, it's got to get better. It just can't, well, it just gets worse. I mean, if you watch it through to the end, you are not even going to believe they released this stuff. I mean, it's like the last Bill Murray movie that was made, by the way, I don't know if you've seen this turkey. He's rocked the Casbah. Again, I'm a Bill Murray guy, right? So I get this on pay-per-view. But now I'm at home, right? 15 minutes, I'm out. I mean, I'm by myself, I'm at home, I got other shit I can go do, right? I drive it all the way to the theater, right? At last, in at least maybe 45 minutes, right? So, because I'd be more invested. So, the more invested you are, the more your optimistic bias kicks in. And therefore, the more reluctant you are to give anything a sword, to admit it's not good. We go. So you've all done this. And I had a great time, so I don't, they're not here, but I don't want them to hear. I mean, I had a wonderful time, and I appreciate being invited, but still. So, clients of mine, Lily Noon and her husband Ed, take Carl and I to the Concourse DeElegance thing at Pebble Beach last year. The big car. It's magnificent, by the way. And you kind of got to dress. I mean, it's not, you know, your neighborhood car show. I mean, it's all outdoors, but most everybody has a jacket, a guy's got a jacket on. You may have a shortsleeve, you know, a white shirt, but he's got a jacket on, and he's got nice slacks, and he's wearing socks. Most of the women are in dresses, I mean, so, you're not dressed up to go to the opera, but you're not, you know, in cargo shorts, and, you know, a T-shirt with holes in it, either. And it is hot. I mean, it is really hot. I mean, it is sweat, sweat, sweat, sweat, sweat, hot. And the thing is sort of, you either get the water, that helps, but it's sort of a bowl, you know, so it's below everything around it, so the heat light gets there and stays, and they're nice, but of course you're using portageons, which the hotter it is, the less appealing that process is. More for women than guys, but still. And it's crowded, and, you know, it's just... So, all day long. Here's what everybody's saying. Well, it's not that bad, right? You know, it's a little warm, but it's not that bad. It's really not that hot, right? It's hotter than Hades, you know? At three o'clock in the afternoon, I'm finally the asshole, right? I'm saying, this is miserable. I mean, it is hotter than hell in here. Let's leave. Oh, they're all waiting for somebody to say, let's leave, right? And nobody wants to be the one. So, this is in people's behavior. And so, when you can leverage sunken cost, optimistic bias, their pre-existing investment, you have a real point of leverage. And most of the time, you ought not be subtle or shy about it. You ought to be kind of blatant. Then there's an interesting issue of misunderstanding why people do things. So, on page 65, from that big, thick book I showed you, so here's what's changed in gambling over two decades. So, it's an evolutionary change. So, if you haven't been lately, and you harken back to slot machines of old, first of all, they all cranks, and they all dispensed money. You had to have a cup to put the coins in. This is all a shock to Jay's people. There were these machines. They actually mechanically pulled a handle, and they dispensed money. So, everybody had big buckets that they had their money in at the casino. Now it's a slip. Yeah, they're at... No, no, I just... I know where they are. Are you...? And really, they were mechanical, right? So, the wheels spun around, and you had to get three pairs on the same line for something to happen. You could still find that machine in most of these regional casinos, but you can only find six or seven of them. So, they were there for somebody who liked 80. The rest of them are really video games. They're made like apps. They're video games. First of all, they don't dispense any money. You get credits. And they are now building some that move the credit in and out of your smartphone. So, you don't even get a piece of paper. You just... You hook up to an app, and the thing puts money in and takes money out of your phone. And they're video games. They work like video games. And a lot more goes on than just wheels spinning around. Little characters, you know, the thing you win bonus rounds, and curtains open, and fireworks go off. The latest thing is a pretend control. So, the latest thing put into these machines is where you are picking. You are not just passive. You're acting. So, you're picking one of three things, and then you see what the prize was behind all three of them. And, of course, you got the shitty prize, but there it was. You could have won 5,000 credits. Like, they didn't move that when you pressed that. I mean, who's kidding who? But you could have, right? There's wheels you spin with your finger. There's a machine where the bonus round is you roll dice with your fingers. And the dice roll, and you get what the dice came up on. It's all nonsense, of course, right? I mean, it's all algorithmed, and it's all... It has nothing to do... But you feel like you're doing something, right? So, these have gotten more and more and more and more sophisticated, has entertained, but... So, now, what the gambling industry is all about, and what the slot machine makers are all about, is not so much about the chance of winning, the illusion of winning. It is about how long a person can stay and play before they run out of money. So, these things are engineered to keep you playing with one more thing that happens, one more thing that happens, one more thing that happens, and really the winning has become irrelevant. Now, at the real addictive end, here's how addictive this is. If you have to pee, you have to leave your machine. So, a problem in the casino business now is people peeing where they are. And this is not a rare and random occurrence. This is a growing problem. Caesars is experimenting with... No, no, no. They are experimenting with personnel that you can pay to take your seat so nobody takes your machine until you come back. So, they are experimenting with roaming people with special jackets on so you know who they are, and you can pay them to keep your seat because you don't want to leave. Now, that used to be because you thought as you walked away, the next person was going to hit the damn jackpot. Now, this is more because you're in the middle of, like a video game, this progressive series of involvements and actions, and you just got to level three, and you're working to get to level four, and you don't want to have to go somewhere and start over. So, it is, and it still has optimistic bias to it, but it has addiction to an enormous degree. So, ideally, we will build addiction into what we do. All sorts of businesses do it, right? So, you guys who run, like, dashboard systems in B2B, you want them coming back every day, and you want some kind of ongoing game, some kind of McDonald's engineers that into their food have for years worse than any other fast food place, by the way, if you don't know it. They add sugar to everything. There is sugar added to every item of McDonald's food. The buns, the stuff the fries are dipped in, the everything. The new cheese sauce, I assure you, is not just cheese sauce, it's cheese and sugar sauce. That's what it is, right, because sugar is addictive. So, you want to think about how your customer experience, how their visits to your place, their visits to your site, how you can get more frequency, more constancy by building in this addictive or nearly addictive feedback loop that makes me want to be there more frequently, more consistently, more constantly, or for longer periods of time. Urgency and loss, which is your number 43. On page 68, I gave you a lost script from Zig. We don't need to go through it, but it's a fine example. And the bottom line is that people are always more reluctant to suffer a loss than they are motivated by getting a gain. It feels different, right? So, we all like to benefit, we all like to gain, we all like to win money, make money, we all like to have a good restaurant experience, whatever the business is, but we dislike loss even more than we appreciate and gain. And you see it played out in virtually every business. So, the way Disney gets compliance with all this advanced booking that you have to do is not by any of the joy of experiencing any of the things you are booking all this time in advance. It's about you not being able to do something if you don't book it all this far in advance. And if that wasn't the case, a bunch of people still wouldn't buy ExpressPass, for example. They wouldn't spend the extra money. If it wasn't drummed into them, you may never get on this thing if you don't have an ExpressPass. And the guy goes, I don't really want to have to explain to these kids why in three days we couldn't do soaring. So, it isn't so much about, gee, I really want to do soaring. It's I don't want to not do soaring. I don't want to have the opportunity taken away from them. Bill wasn't all that excited about doing any of these things until I stopped it now. Now all of a sudden, no shit. I should have gone over there and done this, all right? Because the loss thing is bigger than the benefit thing. And loss always gives you the opportunity to create urgency. Because the fear of loss has it organically. So, my wife is a late sleeper. She's not a morning person, like at all, which is okay. Doesn't matter. But she really does not like getting up in the morning. Pretty much for anything. However, if you're a DVC member, any DVC people in here, Disney Vacation Club? One, me, that's it, really? Next time you're there, go to the pitch. It's worth going for the pitch, by the way. But then some of you will get, well, wind up DVC members. Be sure to mention my name. So, you got a book, so it's the time-share thing, right? So you got a book six months out. If you're going to use your home property, I think it's five, if I'm not mistaken. You know, I think it's five if you're using your home property and six if you're moving around anything else. But whatever it is, let's say it's six. You got a book six months out. You even have a chance of getting exactly what you want. And that means 7 a.m. Eastern Standard Time, the day that is six months out. No shit. Because there's a blue bazillion of them with a stopwatch in their hand, pouncing. And some of them are like Dwight Woods on Diamond Calls. They figured out how to be there ahead of everybody. So for that, Carlos sets her alarm and she's got to be alert. So she sets, she does like it up two minutes before this. She gets up an hour ahead and she does whatever it is they do in the morning. And she has her three cups of coffee and she takes a little walk and she's ready to book this stuff. Because if she's not, we're going to lose out and we're not going to get to be exactly where we want to be, exactly doing the things that we want to be. And they have got her doing this completely contrary to her behavior in any other circumstance. Any other circumstance. This, they got it. And it is totally driven by urgency tied to loss. That's totally what it's driven by. And so people behave this way a lot. If you do coaching calls, you'll hear me do it. If people stopped asking questions, nine out of ten times, if you say, well, I will end early. Once, twice, you don't get to thrice. Somebody's in. Why weren't they in to start with? Why did I have to start to slam the door to get them to move? Because they won't move unless they see the door slamming. That's why it's just the way they are. So when you know that, you want to use it a lot. Now, my one big caution, here's how you get yourself in trouble. You use it with the same constituency, the same audience, and you don't mean it. And you get caught. All you are now is a parent who is threatening, threatening, threatening, and never sending the kid upstairs with no supper. It don't take very long before this has no leverage whatsoever. But if you intend to play fair, it's a very powerful lever. Let's do gaslighting. So gaslighting, and I'm going to give you two specific techniques that aren't in your manual, by the way. Gaslighting is particularly important. It's useful a lot. But it's particularly important when you are selling to somebody who is in some way, shape, or form above you. And or feels like they are superior to you. So at first ten years of my career, I was selling consulting and training, and I was selling, there was nobody I was selling into who wasn't in a better financial position than me. And not that that's all self-evident, it doesn't have to be, but it's certainly in your head, but it's in their head too. I mean, you know, 25-year-old kid, you're selling to a 60-year-old guy who's running a $100 million business, who's kiddin' who, right? So in some way, shape, or form, you have to take somebody down a peg. You have to make them uncertain when they were feeling pretty certain before their defenses start to fray. They have to be knocked off their approach. They have to be surprised suddenly, gee, maybe I'm not as smart as I thought I was. Maybe I'm not as accomplished as I thought I was. And so gaslighting, it's origin to the best of, the origin of the term is from a 1944 movie called Gaslight, if you want to go find it. The plot has been done a million times since. It is a husband who attempts to make his wife believe she is going insane. So plot we've seen a million times, and they call it gaslighting, and ultimately she begins doubting her own sanity, right? So I have a client, for example, who is a wealth advisor who goes after people who are selling or have sold a company, and they have at least $20 million. He's pretty well to do, I don't know if he has $20 million or not, but he's pretty well to do, but that's not the point. The millionaire next door guy, the Stanley millionaire next door guy who has built his business up over time, and he has conquered monsters, and he has survived recessions, and he hit, and now somebody is flattering him by buying his company for what is to him a crapload of money, and he suddenly has a crapload of money. He thinks he's pretty damn smart. And he is, but he's only smart situationally, right? But he thinks he's pretty damn smart. And he is somewhat impervious to advice. And so one of the many things, one of the handful of really stupid things is very common, by the way, for everybody that is in this position. So within the first 12 months, they lose a few million dollars trading stocks on their own when they never hardly traded stocks before. They got time on their hands, they got money, and they think they're real smart. Or they buy into a business they know nothing about. Ego, they need a place to go, they don't feel important anymore, and they think they're really smart. So to get them to not manage their money and hand it over to you, you have got to rattle them, you've got to shake them a little bit. They can't be allowed to continue, they have to be gaslight. And so much of the marketing we do, I'll tell you a couple things, and also you can go see some of it if you want, much of the marketing we do has nothing to do now with what he does, how the money is to be managed, if in fact it is to be managed. A big hunk of it is about affinity, and another big part of it is about gaslighting this guy. So we knock him off his perch and we make him anxious. So like this ad, runs in a number of magazines, it's a free book ad. When a man sells this company conflicting advice flies, vultures circle, unseen peril lurks, and who can really be trusted to tell him the truth. And then this is all about how you can't trust anybody around you. Family, friends, the broker you've had forever, your CPA, etc. So now this guy who actually felt pretty good now doesn't feel so good. This is, the book is 20 million and broke. Losing your fortune, it can happen to anyone. Keeping your fortune can be harder than earning it. Can you keep it? Big anxiety, part of the big anxiety pitch is if not careful, you will put money into, you can put, you will put money that can no longer be replaced into situations with hidden risks. So a big part of this pitch, which is true, but has added virtue of truth, is that business owner builds, his money is captive in his business while he builds it, right? Particularly the millionaire next door type. He takes out a decent salary and once in a while he buys a boat or something, but mostly his money is in the business. It's the first time in his life that he has cash and it can't be replaced. So when you still have your business, if you go blow 50 grand on something, if that's a significant number to you, even if you blow half a million bucks, you still got the oil wells, you still got the pump. You can replace the money. The day you sell the business, all the money you got is irreplaceable. You have no way to replace it. So you lose a dollar, it's gone forever. And that's gaslighting. That's, oh gee, I hadn't thought about that, right? So I'll just read you a couple blocks of copy. Warning, what got you to this dance does not guarantee continued success. Think back to your start in your first business. You made plenty of mistakes, maybe some embarrassing ones. Quite a few entrepreneurs who are ultimately very successful even have a bankruptcy in their past. Now as an ex-company owner with your entire life's work in cash, rather than in your company, you are starting all over again in a new and different endeavor in which you have as little practical experience as you did way back at the start of your first business. Is it really so inconceivable that there are new mistakes to make and that you might make them? Now mistakes can be fatal. The bloody nos and bruises we get and losses we take on the way up get chalked up to education and there's time and youth and opportunity to recover from them. We even wind up nostalgic about our early missteps fond of our battle scars. You and I would enjoy trading war stories, but now there's no time for starting over, no good opportunity to replace the losses. You really do not want to be rolling up your sleeves and returning to work five, seven or ten years from now. Others, last paragraph, others also behave differently. When you were building your business, there were few predators interested in you. Few vultures hanging around because you had no money. But when you exit your company, family members, friends, peers, peddlers of investments and deals materialize, bearing feelings of it's own.
